Facebook really cracked down on this. So some of the tools that we used to be able to use have been a little bit restricted lately, but we can still do some really cool stuff. So if we're looking at something above 800,000 people, really this is what we would consider a large audience size on Facebook. So anything around that $1 million mark is pretty good for Facebook. ## [03:02 - 06:04]

Let's be honest. So I would avoid expanded interests, especially if you're using look likes. What you'll find is that actually will probably stall your campaign if nothing else. If you select expanded interests and look likes. And you would set this up as a conversion objective. You want that algorithm to go after the high intent individuals in this large pool of targeting that you've set out there. Now if you're doing a 40 to 800K audience size, this is kind of ideal for a newer ad account and if you've got an unseasoned pixel. So it's still a fairly large audience size, right? A 400K, 600K is still a fairly large audience size. It's just not the ideal audience size if you have a fairly straightforward product. Bigger audiences are what's working with e-commerce right now. But you can still make this work, especially if it's a new ad account. You might find this audience size is going to work a lot better for you. Then avoid expanded interests and you're setting this one up as well as a conversion objective. And then under 40,000 people. This is pretty small for Facebook. This is what we call a hyper-targeted audience and it's ideal for those kinds of campaigns as well. So for example, if you're trying to go after a specific niche, so you're targeting surgeons in Australia or you want to target the city of Memphis and you've got the word Memphis in the ad, this is what we call a hyper-targeted campaign. Obviously if you target a million people, there's not a million surgeons in Australia, right? So you're going to be spending a lot of money and Facebook's just going to be showing a lot of impressions to people, which is just not relevant to. So this situation is where you really want to hyper-target that audience and go way smaller with it. So condense it much smaller. Now normally for small audiences like this, in the middle and bottom of funnel, you would run this as a different campaign objective than conversions. ## [06:04 - 09:10]

And you're going to want to install that to your browser and it's going to show up like this when it's installed. And we're going to go on Facebook. We used to be able to select custom audiences and you used to be able to bring data up and get Facebook to show you people similar to those that, for example, purchased or added to cart. Unfortunately, this has been removed right under our feet. So we're not able to do that anymore ever since the Cambridge Analytica scandal. So what we can do though is we can basically start off with thinking of what is one competitor of my client or maybe what is a conference or some kind of interest that I know my clients audiences will be attached to, right? So what is one thing that I know my clients audiences will be interested in? So for example, if you're selling monster energy drinks, you could go in here and go Red Bull. I mean, this is a super broad one and select Red Bull. And then if you click on page likes here and you've got the pixel, the plugin installed, you're going to see something show up like this. And essentially what this plugin does is it shows you all of the interests that are available for targeting inside of Facebook ads manager, because obviously not every one of these, even though this is a big brand, for example, it doesn't, it won't actually show up as an interest target. So you don't want to waste your time writing down lists of a whole bunch of things that are not going to show up. But it's basically going to give you different ideas for different targeting brands, public figures, influencers that you can also go after and target. And it's going to show you which ones you can target, which ones you can't. Now, here's an important thing when you're running your tests to your audiences. And I'll repeat this later on as well in case you missed this. But if you are testing on a very, very small budget, so let's say you've only got sort of $200 or $300 for the whole testing phase, what I would recommend is you bulk all your interests together under one ad set, and you essentially allocate most of the budget to that one ad set. However, if you do have more of a budget, a bigger budget to play around with for your testing, so maybe the client has agreed to give you $1,000 or $2,000 for the testing phase, what I would recommend is you almost test out these interests separately in separate ad sets. And what we're finding is working really well right now is if you start off at a $50 ad spend, so daily ad spend. And that seems to be working really, really well. Now, don't forget the whole thing about optimization. Facebook wants to see at least sort of an 8% conversion rate on ads.

## [09:10 - 12:10]

Otherwise, it really impacts how you perform inside of the algorithm. So by the way, that translates to that 50 event triggers that I spoke to you guys about in the algorithm training. And if you're not getting that, you may have to switch, or if your ad sets are not working that well, you may have to switch to the step beforehand in your funnel. So this is just a really cool way to find different audiences and targeting for your interests that you can then use inside your ads manager. Now if we're looking at warm audience sizes, considering this is e-commerce, you're probably going to be running dynamic product ads at the bottom of your funnel, in which case you have no choice to really select that campaign objective regardless of how large that audience size. But typically speaking, an audience size between 20,000 and 100,000 is not really going to be bottom of funnel activity. This is normally your middle of funnel stuff, where it's people that engaged or watched a video, and possibly, possibly depending on the size of the shop, did trigger view content as well. And so you're still running that as a conversion based objective because it's still a fairly large audience. However, anything under 20,000 people, if they don't have enough skews in their product catalog, I would set this up as a post engagement objective campaign if you are not setting it up as a product catalog. So if you do have sort of more than three products in the shop, you're going to want to set up a DPA, a product catalog. Otherwise, just set it up if your client only has like one product, for example, set it up as a post engagement objective. You'll find that that will spend and you'll get a lot more reach with that kind of objective than a DPA. Now, one of the cool things that I want to show you about warm audiences, let's go under audience time. And we're going to click on create audience. And this is something that works really, really well inside of your retargeting stacks, especially for repeat purchase customer strategies. So for example, let's pick add to cart and we can then select frequency here. So for example, if someone's triggered add to cart more than twice, what we can do here is we know they're really hot. They keep coming back to the website. They keep adding stuff to cart. Let's get them to finally bite by giving them an offer by giving them something they can't refuse.

## [12:10 - 13:24]

Maybe you're throwing something on top, maybe you're discounting, or maybe you're even just calling it out like, hey, it's time. It's time that you get this product in your life. So this is a really cool hack, especially inside of your retargeting stacks. You can also do this by purchase. So let's say someone purchased more than twice or three times, they're probably a great customer. They're a loyal customer. So your retargeting campaigns, going after them, should have some different copy in them, calling them out and rewarding them, building loyalty and incentivizing them to buy even more as well. Because if they're an existing high quality customer, they're a lot easier to convert. You'll get a lot higher ROAs than someone that isn't.
